title: Lemma 2.1: algebraic expansion of $e_1MJMJe_2$ and the commuting-square identity $e_1e_2=e$

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Location:** First display in the proof of Lemma 2.1.

The expansion
$$e_1MJMJe_2 = e_1(M\ominus M_1)J(M\ominus M_1)Je_2 + M_1e_1J(M\ominus M_1)Je_2 + JM_1Je_1(M\ominus M_1)e_2 + JM_1JM_1e$$
is presented as immediate. It silently uses:
- the orthogonal splitting $M=(M\ominus M_1)\oplus M_1$ at the $L^2$ level,
- the commutations $e_1M_1=M_1e_1$ and $e_1JM_1J=JM_1Je_1$,
- and crucially the replacement of $e_1\cdots e_2$ by $e$ in the last term, i.e. the identity $e_1M_1JM_1Je_2=JM_1JM_1e$.

The last point is equivalent to the **commuting-square property** $e_1e_2=e_2e_1=e$ (equivalently $E_{M_1}E_{M_2}=E_N$ with $N=\Gamma(\mathcal G_1\cap\mathcal G_2)$) for graph-product subalgebras. This structural fact is never established and underpins the whole computation.

**Required:** prove $e_1e_2=e$ (the commuting square $M_1,M_2,N$) for graph products via the reduced-word decomposition, and then derive each term of the expansion explicitly (including the $e_1\cdots e_2\to e$ collapse).
